confidence.disclosure: Itinerary drawn from the two contemporary Zafarnamas (Yazdi and Shami), Ibn Arabshah's hostile biography, Ruy Gonzalez de Clavijo's eyewitness embassy account, and Beatrice Forbes Manz's modern reconstruction. Timur's entire career is documented with month-level precision in the Zafarnamas, organised into named multi-year campaigns (Three-Year, Five-Year, Seven-Year). Capped at the most historically significant stops of roughly 65 events; thousands of intermediate camps are omitted.

sources: Ibn Arabshah, Aja'ib al-Maqdur fi Nawa'ib Taymur (c. 1435)
Ruy Gonzalez de Clavijo, Embassy to Tamerlane (c. 1406)
Beatrice Forbes Manz, The Rise and Rule of Tamerlane (1989)

journey.event: Born into the Turco-Mongol Barlas tribe at Kesh, south of Samarkand, on 9 April 1336.
Entered the service of the Chagatai prince Tughluq Timur during his invasion of Transoxiana.
Wounded in the right leg and right arm during a campaign in Sistan, leaving him permanently lame ('Timur the Lame').
Defeated his rival Amir Husayn and was proclaimed supreme ruler at Balkh in April 1370.
Made Samarkand his capital and began its transformation into an imperial city.
Launched the first of five campaigns against the Sufi dynasty of Khwarezm.
Campaigned against the Chagatai prince Qamar al-Din in the territory east of Lake Issyk-Kul.
Captured Herat from the Kartid dynasty, beginning the conquest of Khurasan.
Destroyed the irrigation works of Sistan in retribution for resistance.
Subdued the Caspian provinces of Mazandaran and Gurgan.
Began the Three-Year Campaign with the capture of Tabriz from the Jalayirids.
Crossed the Caucasus and sacked the Georgian capital of Tbilisi, capturing King Bagrat V.
Massacred the population of Isfahan in November 1387 after a local revolt; contemporaries report 70,000 skulls piled in towers.
Accepted the submission of the Muzaffarid dynasty at Shiraz.
Returned to Samarkand in response to Tokhtamysh's raids on Transoxiana.
Defeated the Golden Horde khan Tokhtamysh at the Battle of the Kondurcha River in June 1391.
Began the Five-Year Campaign with a second invasion of the Caspian provinces.
Returned to Shiraz and exterminated the Muzaffarid princes; legend holds he met the poet Hafiz here.
Captured Baghdad in October 1393; the Jalayirid sultan Ahmad fled to Mamluk Egypt.
Campaigned in the Jazira and took Diyarbakir and Mardin.
Defeated Tokhtamysh decisively at the Battle of the Terek River, breaking the Golden Horde's power.
Sacked the Golden Horde capital of Sarai on the lower Volga.
Destroyed the city of Hajji Tarkhan (Astrakhan) at the mouth of the Volga.
Returned to Samarkand after four years' campaigning and began major construction projects.
Assembled his army at Kabul for the invasion of India.
Crossed the Indus at Attock in September 1398.
Advance force under his grandson Pir Muhammad took Multan.
Defeated the Tughluq sultan Mahmud Shah II at the Battle of Delhi on 17 December 1398; sacked the city for three days.
Marched northeast to Haridwar on the Ganges before turning back through the Himalayan foothills.
Returned to Samarkand in April 1399 with enormous plunder from India.
Began the Seven-Year Campaign westward from his Azerbaijani base at Tabriz.
Stormed the Ottoman-held city of Sivas in August 1400, burying the Armenian defenders alive.
Took Malatya on the Mamluk frontier.
Defeated the Mamluk army outside Aleppo and sacked the city in November 1400.
Passed through Homs and Hama advancing on Damascus.
Captured Damascus in March 1401 after the Mamluk sultan fled; the city was looted and burned, and artisans deported to Samarkand. Ibn Khaldun met Timur during this siege.
Stormed Baghdad a second time in July 1401 after the Jalayirids reoccupied it; ordered 120 towers built from the skulls of the defenders.
Moved his army toward Anatolia after the Ottoman sultan Bayezid I refused Timurid suzerainty.
Defeated and captured the Ottoman sultan Bayezid I at the Battle of Ankara on 28 July 1402, temporarily shattering the Ottoman state.
Sacked the Ottoman capital of Bursa after Ankara.
Captured Smyrna (Izmir) from the Knights of Rhodes in December 1402 after a two-week siege.
Wintered at Erzurum on the return march east.
Received ambassadors at Sultaniyya, including the Castilian envoy Ruy Gonzalez de Clavijo.
Returned in triumph to Samarkand and received Clavijo and other foreign envoys at a series of grand feasts.
Visited his birthplace Kesh, where his great palace the Aq Saray was under construction.
Set out in November 1404 on his planned invasion of Ming China with 200,000 men.
Fell ill at Otrar on the Syr Darya during the march to China and died on 17 February 1405.
His body was returned to Samarkand and buried in the Gur-i Amir mausoleum.
